You aren't overriding any 'PolicyEngineFeatureAPI' methods, so there is no reason to include it here.

This method appears to be blocking the current thread, until the lock is obtained. If the lock is currently held by another transaction within this Drools session, it won't get a chance to run and free the lock, because this method is holding the one-and-only thread within the Drools session. As a result, this method will loop until the previous user times out, and forcibly take over the lock. Given that the 'sleep' is for 5 seconds, one loop would do it.

You also need 'PolicySessionFeatureAPI' associated with this class, or 'DistributedLockingFeature.globalInit' will not be called. In fact, you really don't need 'PolicyEngineFeatureAPI', because you are not overriding any of its methods.
This is still going to block the thread.

Follow layout as described in the prelude of file:

https://git.onap.org/policy/drools-pdp/tree/policy-management/src/main/server-gen/bin/features

see other existing features for reference as well

create upgrade and downgrade SQL scripts.   Use in the upgrade script for the create table, the "if not exists" to guarantee successful invocation if the table is present.   See other features for reference.

Use same convention for file naming used in other .sql files for features and the drools-pdp source code in general.
Override PolicyEngineFeatureAPI.afterStart() instead of globalInit.    This is too early in the initialization sequence.
Use SystemPersistence.getProperties (see other feature code on how it is done), so you're agnostic to config directory location.

It looks like expiration time is no longer enforced -- locks will remain, regardless of the specified time.
For this feature I was going to create a table within the drools schema. Should the feature have its own schema?
You can use and existing one or a new one.  In any case, since this is particular to a feature, they are feature related resources, and should be organized according to the feature layout directory.

The table will likely have to be audited since a host may go off line while it owns a lock and never release it. I'm not sure how the audit would determine whether or not an owner should still have the lock unless we kept the exipirationTime field in the table. The audit would then remove any rows where the expirationTime > currentTime.

Shouldn't this method always return 'false'? A value of 'true' will prevent other features from having their 'afterStart' method invoked.
